Ecotype_references: Evans 1988: The limited existing Adelosaurus material has different features suggestive of terrestrial and aquatic habits, though its overall morphology makes a terrestrial lifestyle more likely. However, in the absence of more complete material and data, we cannot rule out a Transiently or even Moderately Aquatic lifestyle for this taxon. As a result, its ecotype remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: NA

Ecotype_references: All extant crocodilians are amphibious predators that spend extensive time hunting and wading in the water, but also routinely bask, nest, and travel to some degree on land (Whitaker and Bass 1982; Ferguson 1985; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, ch. 19: Crocodylians; Murray et al. 2020). These species nevertheless span a continuum of semi-aquatic lifestyles, which differ intraspecifically, ontogenetically, and depending on weather and other external factors. In order to bin crocodilians by ecotype in an analysis that could plausibly encompass all other amniotes, we inevitably oversimplify the complex natural history of the clade. We try only to score them in a consistent way that broadly reflects their ecological history permits comparison to other amniotes. To that end, we describe our reasoning for these scores. We consider the brevirostrine species Osteolaemus tetraspis and Paleosuchus trigonatus the least aquatic crocodilians. O. tetraspis often inhabits sparsely inundated shallow inland streams and cave systems (Crocodile Specialist Group 1996; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 332: Crocodylidae; Shirley et al. 2017), and it regularly hunts small terrestrial prey (Pauwels et al. 2007), which may make up the entirety of its adult diet in cave-dwelling populations (Shirley et al. 2017). Though quantitative comparisons of water and land habitation are sparse, one study found that some (but not all) captive O. tetraspis individuals spent the overwhelming majority of their time on land (Uwakaneme et al. 2004 [abstract])—a finding consistent with the highly terrestrial diet of some populations (Shirley et al. 2017). P. trigonatus is likewise often found about small inland streams in heavily forested areas (Medem 1958; Magnusson et al. 1987; Brochu 2001); adults consume primarily terrestrial prey (Jackson et al. 1974; Magnusson et al. 1987), and spens vast stretches of time on land, with one wild individual apparently residing under dry ground for 10 days (Magnusson and Lima 1990). These relatively terrestrial habits have led some authors to suggest a connection between terrestriality and brevirostry (Kälin 1933, cited in Medem 1958; Brochu 2001). However, the closely related P. palpebrosus, which is more conspicuously brevirostrine than its congener (Medem 1958), resides about larger water bodies and consumes relatively less terrestrial prey (Magnusson et al. 1987), casting doubt on this suggestion. Regardless, these species still typically spend extended periods of time in the water and have at least partly aquatic diets. As a result, we consider P. trigonatus and O. tetraspis Moderately Aquatic, with equal-to-subequal regions of niche space and habitation time devoted to land and water. In contrast, we consider P. palpebrosus and all other extant crocodilian species Highly Aquatic, as most individuals seem to spend the majority of their time in the water, coming onto land only to bask and nest. Within this single bin of “Highly Aquatic” crocodilians, we stress the existence of a finer continuum. In particular, we might well consider gavialids and the Saltwater Crocodile more aquatic than the other species sorted within this bin. Perhaps because they descend from marine ancestors (Wilberg et al. 2019), the gavialids G. gangeticus and T. schlegelli have longirostrine snouts often treated as specializations for hunting fish, though their diet is really more varied (Whitaker and Basu 1982). And while T. schlegelli occupies lentic marsh waters (Staniewicz 2018), G. gangeticus resides in deep, fast-moving rivers, seldom (if ever) moves far from the water’s edge, often remains submerged during nest attendance, and has more limited mobility on land than other modern crocodilians (Whitaker and Bass 1982; Vitt and Caldwell 2014: Gavialidae, p. 548; Choudhary et al. 2017; Murray et al. 2020). As a result, G. gangeticus is often considered the most aquatic modern crocodilian (Vitt and Caldwell 2014: Gavialidae, p. 548). In a similar vein, the  Crocodylus porosus may be the most active swimmer among modern crocodilians, often making extended (>1000 km) journeys over open ocean (Fukuda et al. 2019; Spennerman 2020). As a result, one might draw finer ecotypic distinctions among the many less terrestrial crocodilians. For this study, given that gavialids, C. porosus, and all their potentially less aquatic counterparts seem to spend the majority of their time in the water, but periodically return to land to nest, rest, and bask, we consider them all Highly Aquatic. In brief, we treat O. tetraspis and P. trigonatus as Moderately Aquatic, and all other species as Highly so.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimens YPM_R_06531 and YPM_R_06535, and taxidermied specimen YPM_R_15068

Ecotype_references: Larivière 2003; Wright et al. 2021: Although primarily aquatic, Aonyx cinerea regularly spends extensive time on land, and will occasionally travel far from water sources.
Limb_type_references: AMNH skin specimens of Amblyonyx spp.

Ecotype_references: Stokes et al. 2004; Marchesan and Carthew 2008: This species is predominantly ground-dwelling. We find no reference to aquatic habits.
Limb_type_references: No zoological specimens were available for us to examine. As a result, its webbing phenotype remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: Although clearly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Romer 1956, Osteology of the Reptiles, pp. 344, 524; Rieppel et al. 2005), thalattosaurs seem to retain well-developed jointed limbs, suggesting that they retained the ability to make limited trips on land. The degree to which they traveled onto land remains unclear, so we cannot resolve whether thalattosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: NA

Ecotype_references: A. macrurus is a fossorial ground-dwelling species (Molur 2020). The closely related A. africanus is apparently a good swimmer but rarely forays into the water (Jori et al. 1988), and we find no reference to regular aquatic habits in either species. As a result, we consider A. macrurus Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen AMNH_59922

Ecotype_references: Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531: Cheloniidae

Ecotype_references: Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 528: Chelidae

Ecotype_references: The Pygmy Hippopotamus (Choeropsis liberiensis) might be considered less aquatic than its larger counterpart (Hippopotamus amphibius), as it gives birth on land—although less is known about its natural history (Ransom et al. 2015). However, its range is limited to marshes and water courses, and it still spends extensive time in an aquatic medium, foraging for semi-aquatic plants, suckling pups, and (in some cases at least) copulating in the water. As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Hippopotamids were scored using fluid-preserved Choeropsis specimens AMNH_214396 and AMNH_146608, which had very slight webbing between D3 and D4.

Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest that Claudiosaurus had an aquatic lifestyle (Carroll 1981; Sues 2019, p. 41). However, it retains well-developed jointed appendages, and it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. As a result, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was Moderately or Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: NA

Ecotype_references: Petersen and Yates 1980; Reid 2006 (Star-Nosed Mole: pp. 389–390): This talpid is an eager and excellent swimmer, and often has a diet often consisting primarily of aquatic prey. It typically inhabits swamps, streams, or the wetter regions of woods or meadows. However, it seems to spend extensive time on land as well, foraging commonly (and, in some cases, primarily) for terrestrial prey, and building nests far above the water line using terrestrial vegetation. As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM_04525 and YPM_02199: hands unwebbed but with stiff paradigital scales; feet partially webbed

Ecotype_references: Vitt and Caldwell, p. 590: Corytophanidae; Bolívar et al. 2016: These lizards are described as a highly arboreal species occupying terrestrial habitats.
Limb_type_references: PENDING

Ecotype_references: Browne et al. 2014; Vitt and Caldwell 2014 (Cryptobranchidae, pp. 459–460); IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group 2022: Cryptobranchids are fully aquatic salamanders that reproduce, spawn, metamorphose, and spend most if not all of their time as an adult submerged. It respires primarily through its skin in the water. The major staple of its diet is freshwater crayfish, and while it can presumably leave the water for vanishingly short periods, we find no reports of extensive overland travel.
Limb_type_references: PENDING

Ecotype_references: Berta 2008; Bowen 2008: Pinnipeds are highly adapted to aquatic life, and spend most of their time in the water. They are active swimmers and dive to forage for their exclusively aquatic prey. However, they also routinely haul onto land to rest, bask, and (in some species more than others) court and breed. As a result, we consider all pinnipeds Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Cooper 2008, Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy. This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH 201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved).

Ecotype_references: Didelphis species are strong but inefficient swimmers, which will readily take to the water, even as an occasional means of escape, but tire and chill quickly when submerged (Doubt 1974; McManus 1974; Fish 1993). Given the presumed rare nature of this occurrence, we follow Fish (1993) in calling the Virginia opossum “Terrestrial”. However, if the use of water as a means of escape were common, we would call the species “Transiently Aquatic”
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM_14365, YPM_15326, and YPM_15327 had extensive palm girth overhang, particularly on hands, but no true interdigital webbing.

Ecotype_references: Bernal 2016; Roach 2016: Maras (Dolichotis spp.) occupy terrestrial environments. We find no reference to aquatic habits.
Limb_type_references: AMNH skin specimens of Dolichotis spp. were unwebbed.

Ecotype_references: Reid 2006, pp. 472–473: Sea Otter: This highly aquatic species seldom leaves the water, only venturing onto land for short periods during intense storms.
Limb_type_references: Hands have moderate complete webbing. Feet have complete, extensive webbing. The feet are so highly webbed as to be near-flippered. Indeed, Reid (2006, p. 472) calls their hindlimbs flippers, but we score them as non-flippered, given that the webbing does not functionally integrate the auto- and zeugopodia into a continuous unit. Scored based on high-resolution photographs provided by Michael Durham (Oregon Zoo, Oregon, USA)

Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest a predominantly terrestrial lifestyle in this taxon, but leave open the possibility of occasional forays into the water (Scheyer et al. 2017). As a result, we cannot resolve whether this taxon is Terrestrial or Transiently Aquatic.

Ecotype_references: The first complete skeleton of Heterodontosaurus was uncovered from the Upper Red Beds of the Stormberg Group in South Africa (Santa Luca et al. 1976), which represent a distinctly terrestrial (arid or semi-arid) paleoenvironment. It has a relatively elongate manus and pes, but these seem adapted for grasping and running, respectively (Santa Luca et al. 1976; Norman et al. 2012). As a result, Heterodontosaurus is probably terrestrial, and we score it accordingly. However, in the additional data, we cannot rule out occasional forays into an aquatic medium, and this scoring remains tentative.
Limb_type_references: NA

Ecotype_references: The Common Hippopotamus is well-adapted to a semi-aquatic lifestyle (Van Der Geer et al. 2014). It spends the majority of the day standing or bottom-walking in the shallow water of rivers, coastal lakes, and marine estuaries (Fisher et al. 2007; Lewison and Pluhácek 2017; Endo et al. 2018). They typically leave the water each night to forage on land for terrestrial grasses, which comprise a large part of their diet (Lewison and Pluhácek 2017). They have also been known to travel several kilometers overland at night (Clarke 1953). Given its extended daily activity both in water and on land, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Hippopotamids were scored using fluid-preserved Choeropsis specimens AMNH_214396 and AMNH_146608, which had very slight webbing between D3 and D4. However, Hippopotamus may have somewhat more pronounced webbing, and as we could not directly observe any zoological Hippopotamus specimens, this scoring remains tentative.

Ecotype_references: These lizards often use water as a means of escape, and have clear morphological adaptations to suit this purpose (Bauer and Jackman 2008). However, while individuals have been observed residing in a river, they are most often observed and mainly reside on land (Denzer et al. 2020; O’Shea et al. 2021 IUCN assessment for Hydrosaurus amboinensis). As a result, we treat them as “Transiently Aquatic”. Reasonably, however, Watkins-Colwell (1994) calls these lizards “routinely semiaquatic” (p. 24), and a “Moderately Aquatic” designation may be preferred depending on the frequency of their submerged activity. In the absence of additional behavioral data, we assume recurring but typically short forays into the water for this species, which then tentatively merits a “Transiently Aquatic” designation.
Limb_type_references: Hydrosaurus spp. scored from fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_12886 (Hydrosaurus_amboinensis). Forelimb is unwebbed and lacking paradigital integument. Hindlimb is either lobate-webbed or possessing paradigital integument (ie: scales). Resolving which score would be most accurate requires additional study (dissection and/or developmental work), so hindlimb webbing score was marked as NA for the present paper.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1949, p. 73; Thewissen and Taylor 2007; Griebeler and Klein 2019. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs as “(Almost) Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Motani 2005; Gutarra and Rahman 2021)

Limb_type_references: Skin specimens AMNH_54008 and AMNH_33331

Ecotype_references: Although primarily aquatic, Lutra spp. spend extensive time out of the water and occasionally make extended overland journeys between water courses (Carss 1995; Janice and Giere 2010; Sasaki et al. 2021; Loy et al. 2022). As a result, we consider them Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Lutra spp. scorings based on fluid-preserved specimen AMNH_202460

Ecotype_references: Ellis et al. 2016: This species inhabits arid or semi-arid environments. We find no reference to aquatic habits.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM MAM 31 (Macropus eugenii): palm girth but no webbing; YPM MAM 04100 (Macropus eugenii): no webbing; YPM MAM 32 (Macropus canguru/giganteus: clearly unwebbed. No Macropus Rufus skin specimens available, so scoring of M. rufus is tentative, based solely on webbing phenotype of congeners.

Ecotype_references: This predominantly arboreal species resides near water courses (Pietersen et al. 2019) and is apparently a good swimmer (Jansen et al. 2020). However, while swimming is more common in some other pangolin species, like Phataginus tetradactyla (Gudehus et al. 2020) and Smutsia gigantea (de Heusch et al. 1993), one non-biological report suggests that P. tricuspis seldom enters the water (de Heusch et al. 1993). In the absence of additional natural history data to clarify this claim, we cannot resolve whether the species is Transiently Aquatic or Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen of Phataginus tricuspis was missing legs, so we based the webbing phenotype of this species on the closely related skin specimen YPM_04415 (Manis_tetradactyla), whose hands and feet were both unwebbed.

Ecotype_references: Reid 2006 (Cetacea: p. 510); Chivers 2008: Cetaceans are fully aquatic mammals, bearing their young and spending their entirely lives in the water.
Limb_type_references: All crown cetaceans were scored as having fully flippered forelimbs, following Cooper 2008 Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimens AMNH_202413 (Megaptera_novaeangliae), AMNH_239112 (Stenella_frontalis), AMNH_200667 (Monodon_monoceros), and the assessment of soft-tissue outlines from all sampled USNM radiographs.

Ecotype_references: Brito et al. 2021: This arboreal species spends the majority of its time in trees and the rest of its time on the ground. We find no reference to aquatic habits.
Limb_type_references: No zoological specimens were available for us to examine. As a result, its webbing phenotype remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: Doughty et al. 2017
Reid 2006 (Cetacea: p. 510); Chivers 2008: Cetaceans are fully aquatic mammals, bearing their young and spending their entirely lives in the water.
Limb_type_references: All crown cetaceans were scored as having fully flippered forelimbs, following Cooper 2008 Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimens AMNH_202413 (Megaptera_novaeangliae), AMNH_239112 (Stenella_frontalis), AMNH_200667 (Monodon_monoceros), and the assessment of soft-tissue outlines from all sampled USNM radiographs.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1949, p. 73; Thewissen and Taylor 2007; Griebeler and Klein 2019. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs as “(Almost) Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Plesiosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Ketchum and Benson 2010; Gutarra and Rahman 2021). Even when these limbs are described as “flipperlike” (Robinson 1975) or “paddle-like” (Caldwell 2002), they are treated as archetypal flippers.

Ecotype_references: The aquatic affinity of the European Polecat is a little unclear (Blandford 1987). Some sources report that it avoids and refrains from entering water, while others highlight its great swimming ability and high affinity for water (reviewed in Blandford 1987). These reports suggest intraspecific and perhaps geographic variation in polecat habitat preferences (Blandford 1987). Because there are nevertheless reports of these animals readily traversing water and consuming some aquatic prey (Blandford 1987), we consider the species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens AMNH_243106, AMNH_36632, and YPM_01479: possible slight webbing between pD3 and pD4, but very difficult to tell.

Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003; Griebeler and Klein 2019), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003; Griebeler and Klein 2019). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend most of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Highly Aquatic or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: NA

Ecotype_references: MacDonald and Jones 1987; Reid 2006 (Collared Pika: pp. 341–342): We find no reference to aquatic habits in this species.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen YPM_14833: hands and feet unwebbed

Ecotype_references: Fay 1982; Reid 2006, pp. 475–476, Walrus: Though they spend the majority (a ball-parked “two-thirds”) of their time in the water (Fay 1982), walruses also frequently haul out onto land to court mates, rest, bask, and give birth. They have limited terrestrial mobility, hunting and mating almost exclusively in the water.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Cooper 2008, Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy. This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH 201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved).

Ecotype_references: Berta 2008; Bowen 2008: Pinnipeds are highly adapted to aquatic life, and spend most of their time in the water. They are active swimmers and dive to forage for their exclusively aquatic prey. However, they also routinely haul onto land to rest, bask, and (in some species more than others) court and breed. As a result, we consider all pinnipeds Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (following Cooper 2008, Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy). This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH 201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved).

Ecotype_references: Pleurosaurs are highly adapted for an aquatic lifestyle (Dupret 2004; Jones 2008; Bever and Norell 2017). The extreme reduction of the limbs and elongation of the body cast doubt on their ability to travel on land for extended periods. However, we have no data to resolve whether pleurosaurs were Highly or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic, so their ecotype remains ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: NA

Ecotype_references: The majority of gekkonid species typically dwell on rocks, in trees, or on/under terrestrial substrates (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 562: Gekkonidae). Researchers have reported a transiently aquatic lifestyle in just one gekkonid species: Hemidactylus platyurus, which has been observed to commonly employ a unique swimming gait to travel between trees (Nirody et al. 2018). However, to our knowledge, no comparable forays into water have been described for Ptychozoon spp. Though Ptychozoon lionotum inhabits moist tropical forest lowlands, it is considered terrestrial by the IUCN (Sumontha et al. 2008).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_14447

Ecotype_references: It remains disputed whether and to what extent Saniwa was semi-aquatic (Rieppel and Grande 2007, and references therein).
Limb_type_references: NA
Notes_on_occurrences: Only those entries for Saniwa ensidens were used to determine this range.

Ecotype_references: Although mesosaurs were viviparous (Piñeiro et al. 2012) and clearly adapted for aquatic life (Romer 1956; Demarco et al. 2018), aspects of their vertebral morphometry have alluded to a more semi-aquatic lifestyle, with considerable time spent both in water and on land (Demarco et al. 2019). However, we lack direct evidence concerning whether and to what extent they actually traversed on land, so it remains unclear whether mesosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All mesosaurids were scored as having extensive, complete pedal webbing, based on exceptional soft-tissues preserved on the hindlimb skeletons of the following mesosaurid specimens: SMNK_PAL_3806, SMF_R_4710, BSPG_1964_I_268, and URCR_064. Scorings were based on high-resolution photographs of the specimens, provided by Antoine Vérriere. Less soft-tissue preservation was discernible on forelimbs, but manual webbing phenotype was tentatively scored based on specimen NHMW_1987_0067_0001, which appears to have complete extensive webbing. Because the taxonomic breadth covered by these specimens remains unclear, the application of these scores to all sampled mesosaurids remains a tentative first approximation, as mesosaurids may have more variability in webbing phenotype than we can currently discern based on available specimens.

Ecotype_references: Rismiller and Grutzner 2017: Although it is primarily a fossorial ground-dweller, Tachyglossus is also good swimmer and will readily cross natural waterways. As a result, we consider the species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Extensive palm girth overhang with at least partial webbing (tentative scoring based on taxidermied specimen YPM 10903).

Ecotype_references: Although this species occupies terrestrial environments (Reid 2006: American Badger, pp. 470–471), the American Badger is a good swimmer (Long 1973), and at least two observations have clarified that it will readily travel great distances over water (Wood 1921; Duebbert 1967), for reasons that remain unclear. Assuming this behavior is infrequent but typical, we consider the American Badger to be Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Taxidea spp. were scored based on “Taxidea americana” skin specimen AMNH_02842, which appeared unwebbed. However, Long (1973) reports that badgers have a partially webbed manus (and presumably unwebbed feet). This description of the manus conflicts with our observation on skin specimens, so we score the hand’s webbing phenotype as “Unclear”.

Ecotype_references: Shea 2017
Limb_type_references: Taxidermied Tiliqua_scincoides specimen YPM_HERR_17293: completely unwebbed

Ecotype_references: Domning 2008: Sirenians are fully aquatic. They are unable to travel on land, bearing their young and spending their entire lives in the water.
Limb_type_references: All sirenians have fully flippered forelimbs (Cooper 2008). This was supported by direct observation of Trichechus specimens (AMNH S-03: Trichechus_inunguis, fluid-preserved; AMNH_202525 and AMNH_201322: Trichechus_sp, fluid-preserved) and high-resolution photographs of Trichechus manatus latirostris in the wild, provided by Meghan Cohorst: Save the Manatee Club, FL, USA).

Ecotype_references: Reid 2006 (Cetacea: p. 510); Chivers 2008: Cetaceans are fully aquatic mammals, bearing their young and spending their entirely lives in the water.
Limb_type_references: All crown cetaceans were scored as having fully flippered forelimbs, following Cooper 2008 Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimens AMNH_202413 (Megaptera_novaeangliae), AMNH_239112 (Stenella_frontalis), AMNH_200667 (Monodon_monoceros), and the assessment of soft-tissue outlines from all sampled USNM radiographs.

Ecotype_references: Reid 2006 (Cetacea: p. 510); Chivers 2008: Cetaceans are fully aquatic mammals, bearing their young and spending their entirely lives in the water.
Limb_type_references: All crown cetaceans were scored as having fully flippered forelimbs, following Cooper 2008 Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimens AMNH_202413 (Megaptera_novaeangliae), AMNH_239112 (Stenella_frontalis), AMNH_200667 (Monodon_monoceros), and the assessment of soft-tissue outlines from all sampled USNM radiographs.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1949, p. 73; Thewissen and Taylor 2007; Griebeler and Klein 2019. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs as “(Almost) Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Mosasaurs are typically described as having flippers (eg, Caldwell 2002; Lindgren et al. 2013). Even when these limbs are described as “paddle-like” (Caldwell 2002), they are treated as archetypal flippers or paddles.

Ecotype_references: Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 586: Leiolepidinae: All leiolepidine species are described as fully terrestrial.

Berta 2008; Bowen 2008: Pinnipeds are highly adapted to aquatic life, and spend most of their time in the water. They are active swimmers and dive to forage for their exclusively aquatic prey. However, they also routinely haul onto land to rest, bask, and (in some species more than others) court and breed. As a result, we consider all pinnipeds Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Cooper 2008, Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals entries on limb anatomy. This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH 201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved).
